Page 28, Section 4 (Conclusions, Connections, and Comparisons)

To conclude, we stress that the analysis we did in this paper is only about bounding the number of edge steps and non-degenerate pivots.
Question 2. Is there a polynomial bound to the number of degenerate pivots performed by any of the pivot rules we presented when applied to 0/1-LPs?
title: Polynomial bound on degenerate simplex pivots for 0/1 polytope linear programs
background_latex:
Consider a linear program over a fixed feasible region that is a 0/1 polytope:
\[
\max\{c^\top x : x \in P\},\qquad P=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n: Ax=b,\; Dx\le d\},
\]
where all extreme points (vertices) of \(P\) have coordinates in \(\{0,1\}\), and the input data \(A,b,D,d,c\) are integral.

To run the Simplex method, one may convert the LP to standard equality form by introducing slack variables (without requiring those slack variables to be 0/1 at vertices):
\[
\max\{(c')^\top x' : A' x' = b',\; x'\ge 0\},
\]
with \(A'\in\mathbb{Z}^{m'\times n'}\), \(b'\in\mathbb{Z}^{m'}\), \(c'\in\mathbb{Z}^{n'}\). A feasible basis \(B\subseteq [n']\) of size \(m'\) determines a basic feasible solution (BFS) \(x'\). A simplex pivot exchanges one entering nonbasic variable with one leaving basic variable, producing a new basis (and a new BFS).

A pivot is called non-degenerate if it changes the current vertex (equivalently, the step length in the pivot direction is positive), and degenerate if the basis changes but the BFS remains at the same vertex (step length zero). Degeneracy can cause cycling or long sequences of degenerate pivots (stalling).

The paper introduces specific pivot rules for 0/1-LPs (variants of steepest-edge and shadow rules) and proves polynomial bounds on the number of non-degenerate pivots; however, the number of degenerate pivots (basis exchanges that do not move to a different vertex) may still be large and is not bounded in their analysis.
problem_definition_latex:
For at least one of the pivot rules introduced for 0/1-LPs (e.g., the paper's modified steepest-edge or modified shadow pivot rules), determine whether there exists a polynomial upper bound (in the input size, or at least in \(n,m'\)) on the total number of degenerate pivots (degenerate basis exchanges) that the Simplex method can perform when solving
\[
\max\{c^\top x : x \in P\},\qquad P\text{ a 0/1 polytope as above}.
\]
Equivalently, bound by a polynomial the number of iterations in which the basis changes but the current BFS (and hence the current vertex of \(P\)) does not change, when the Simplex method is run with one of these pivot rules on arbitrary 0/1-LPs.

open_problem_quote:
To conclude, we stress that the analysis we did in this paper is only about bounding the number of edge steps and non-degenerate pivots.
Question 2. Is there a polynomial bound to the number of degenerate pivots performed by any of the pivot rules we presented when applied to 0/1-LPs?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Given a concrete pivot rule, one can computationally generate families of 0/1-LPs and simulate the pivot sequence to search for instances with many degenerate pivots (stalling). Detecting degeneracy and counting degenerate basis exchanges is straightforward during simulation, enabling large-scale automated searches for superpolynomial growth patterns.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate counterexample families (constraint matrices/objectives) can be programmatically parameterized, and a fitness signal can be the measured number of degenerate pivots under the specified pivot rule. An evolutionary loop can mutate instances to maximize stalling while preserving the 0/1-vertex property (or approximating it with verifiable checks on small sizes).

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper of Black–De Loera–Kafer–Sanità (2021) introduces three pivot rules tailored to 0/1 polytopes—True Steepest-Edge, Slim Shadow, and Ordered Shadow—and proves strongly polynomial bounds on the number of non-degenerate pivots (edge moves on the 1-skeleton). The key geometric input is that at a 0/1 vertex the feasible cone lies in a single orthant, allowing the 1-norm steepness to be expressed via \(v=1-2x\) and enabling short coherent shadow paths when \(v\) is chosen to take few values on \(\{0,1\}^n\). However, the analysis explicitly does not control degenerate pivots: basis changes that keep the same BFS/vertex, which can cause stalling even when the underlying edge path is short.

No forward-citation evidence was provided, and within the cited background literature there is likewise no known polynomial upper bound on the number of degenerate pivots for these (or any) deterministic pivot rules in full generality. Standard anti-cycling devices such as lexicographic pivoting (Terlaky; Murty) guarantee termination but not polynomially many degenerate steps. Complexity-theoretic results on pivot rules (Adler–Papadimitriou–Rubinstein; Disser–Skutella) underscore why bounding stalling is difficult. Parameterized bounds on the number of distinct BFSs (Kitahara–Mizuno) apply to restricted standard-form 0/1 settings but do not extend to arbitrary 0/1 polytopes after adding slack variables, and they do not yield a polynomial bound on degenerate basis exchanges.

Consequently, the problem of proving a polynomial bound on the total number of degenerate pivots for at least one of the new 0/1-polytope pivot rules remains open. Promising directions suggested by the source paper’s framework include exploiting the special structure of the basic cones \(C(B)\) relative to feasible cones \(C(x)\), and proving that the auxiliary potential \(v^\top z\) (or the shadow slope \(c^\top z / v^\top z\)) decreases in a way that limits how many degenerate bases can occur at a fixed vertex, perhaps via bounds on the number of distinct basic cones compatible with a given 0/1 vertex.

sources.title: On the Simplex method for 0/1 polytopes
sources.theorem_or_result:
Introduces the True Steepest-Edge, Slim Shadow, and Ordered Shadow pivot rules for LPs over 0/1 polytopes and proves strongly polynomial bounds on the number of non-degenerate pivots (at most 
- strongly polynomial for True Steepest-Edge via steepest-edge paths,
- at most \(n\) for Slim Shadow,
- at most \(d\) (dimension) for Ordered Shadow).
It explicitly leaves open whether there is a polynomial bound on the number of degenerate pivots (stalling) for these rules.
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open question: whether any of the proposed pivot rules admits a polynomial upper bound on the number of degenerate pivots (basis changes with zero step length) when solving arbitrary 0/1-LPs without perturbing away the 0/1 structure.

sources.title: Complexity of degeneracy
sources.theorem_or_result: Surveys how degeneracy leads to cycling and stalling in the simplex method and discusses why bounding degenerate pivots is closely tied to the broader open problem of finding polynomial pivot rules; provides context and known barriers rather than a polynomial bound.
sources.connection: Provides background on why degenerate pivot counts are hard to bound in general and frames the 0/1-polytope question as a special case where perturbation is undesirable.

sources.title: Geometry of the Gass-Saaty parametric cost LP algorithm
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies geometric behavior of the Gass–Saaty/parametric (shadow) simplex method under degeneracy, giving structural insights into pivot paths and degeneracy handling, but not a general polynomial bound on degenerate pivots.
sources.connection: Relevant to the modified shadow rules in the source paper and to understanding how degeneracy interacts with shadow/parametric pivoting, though it does not resolve polynomial stalling bounds.

sources.title: Lexicographic pivoting rules
sources.theorem_or_result: Describes lexicographic tie-breaking rules that prevent cycling in simplex under degeneracy; ensures termination but does not guarantee polynomial bounds on the number of degenerate pivots.
sources.connection: The source paper uses lexicographic leaving-variable rules to avoid cycling; this reference provides the standard anti-cycling mechanism but highlights that stalling can remain exponential.

sources.title: On simplex pivoting rules and complexity theory
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows complexity-theoretic hardness phenomena for simplex pivoting rules (viewed as computational problems), supporting the view that strong worst-case guarantees for pivot rules are difficult.
sources.connection: Gives theoretical evidence that bounding pivot behavior (including degeneracy/stalling) may be hard even for structured settings, motivating why the 0/1-polytope degeneracy bound remains open.

sources.title: The simplex algorithm is NP-mighty
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes NP-hardness-type results for predicting/controlling simplex behavior, reinforcing limits on what can be efficiently guaranteed for pivot rules in general.
sources.connection: Supports the broader barrier: even deciding properties of simplex runs can be hard; while not specific to 0/1 polytopes, it contextualizes why polynomial bounds on degenerate pivots are elusive.

sources.title: A bound for the number of different basic solutions generated by the simplex method
sources.theorem_or_result: Bounds the number of distinct basic feasible solutions generated by simplex with Dantzig's rule in terms of parameters \(\tau,\gamma\) (minimum/maximum positive BFS entries), yielding strongly polynomial bounds for certain classes where all variables at vertices are in \(\{0,1\}\) in standard form.
sources.connection: Shows that for special 0/1-LPs that remain 0/1 after conversion to standard form (no large slack values), one can control the number of (non-degenerate) BFS changes; however it does not bound degenerate pivots and does not apply to general 0/1 polytopes after adding slacks.
